title: Catechist's Guide — Man and Sin
html:
Catechist's Guide

For class preparation and delivery. For the catechist's use only.

Class Summary

Estimated duration: 60-75 minutes

Central theme: Created in God's image for communion, humanity fell through sin — not a mere rule-breaking but a rupture of relationship. Original sin is transmitted by propagation, not imitation: a state of broken communion that wounds but does not destroy the divine image. From the very moment of the Fall, God promised a Redeemer. The long defeat of sin is answered by the eucatastrophe of grace: where sin increased, grace abounded all the more.

Objectives:

  • Understand that the human being is created in God's image, with reason, freedom, and the capacity to love, and is made for communion — with God, with others, and with creation.
  • Grasp the unity of body and soul as essential to human dignity: we are not spirits trapped in bodies but embodied persons destined for resurrection.
  • Enter deeply into the Genesis 3 narrative: the serpent's distortion strategy, the dynamics of temptation, the decision to be «like God without God,» and the devastating consequences of sin.
  • Understand original sin not as inherited guilt but as a state of broken communion transmitted by propagation, with concupiscence as its lasting wound.
  • Embrace the Protoevangelium (Gen 3:15) as the first Gospel: the promise that the woman's offspring will crush the serpent's head, fulfilled in Christ and prefigured in Mary.
  • Experience the asymmetry of grace: «where sin increased, grace abounded all the more» (Rom 5:20).
  • Connect the theology of sin to cultural expressions — Milton, Tolkien, Michelangelo, Guadalupe, The Exorcist — and to their own lives.

Key verse: «Where sin increased, grace abounded all the more» (Rom 5:20)

CCC references: 355-421

Suggested Class Structure

Section Duration What the catechist does
1. Opening — Big Question & The Long Defeat 10-12 min Open with Dostoevsky's Ivan Karamazov: «If God is good, why do children suffer?» Then introduce Tolkien's «long defeat» and «eucatastrophe» as a framework for the whole class. Ask: Where do you feel the long defeat in your own life?
2. Created in God's Image / Body and Soul 10 min Genesis 1:26-27. The three capacities: reason, freedom, love. The «Hands» seed-box activity. Unity of body and soul: why the body matters. Psalm 8 connection.
3. Male and Female / The Fall Narrative 15 min Equal dignity and complementarity (Genesis 1:27, CCC 369-373). Then read or dramatically narrate Genesis 3:1-13 aloud. Walk through each step: the serpent's distortion, Eve's addition to God's word (3:3), the threefold appeal (good for food, delight to eyes, desirable for wisdom), the eating, the hiding, «Where are you?» Emphasize: the core is wanting to be like God without God.
4. Original Sin, Concupiscence & Consequences 10 min Original sin as state of broken communion, transmitted by propagation. Concupiscence as the lasting wound (Romans 7:15-19). The fourfold rupture of relationships. Augustine: «I was made and I unmade myself.»
5. Protoevangelium & Mary as New Eve 10 min Genesis 3:15 as first Gospel. Connect to Luke 1:26-38: Eve's no / Mary's yes. The Adam-Christ typology (Romans 5:12-21). O felix culpa. Introduce Our Lady of Guadalupe as the Woman crushing the serpent. Show an image if possible.
6. For Reflection 8 min Participants discuss 1-2 of the reflection questions in small groups (2-3 people), then brief plenary sharing. Option: focus on Q3 («Where are you?») and Q5 (eucatastrophe).
7. Closing: Key Idea + Prayer 5 min Read the Key Idea callout together. Close with the weekly prayer from the workbook, said together. End on the asymmetry of grace.
8. Week Assignment 3 min Distribute workbook. Highlight: Letter to God («Lord, I hid myself because...»), the Broken Mirror drawing, and the «Did God Really Say?» journal.

Notes for the Catechist

The Long Defeat and Eucatastrophe: A Framework for the Whole Class

These two Tolkien concepts are not decorative literary references; they are a powerful catechetical framework. The long defeat names the experience of original sin across generations: the sense that we are caught in patterns we did not choose, wounds we inherited, evils that seem to repeat endlessly. The eucatastrophe names the surprise of grace: the sudden turn when all seemed lost. Frame the class around this arc. Humanity's story is a long defeat from Adam until Christ — with glimmers of eucatastrophe along the way (the covenant with Noah, the call of Abraham, the Exodus, the prophets) — and then the definitive eucatastrophe of the Cross and Resurrection. Use this language; it is poetic, memorable, and deeply true. If your group is literary, point to specific moments in The Silmarillion or The Lord of the Rings; the connection to the Christian story is intentional and powerful.

The Serpent's Strategy: Distortion, Not Denial

The serpent does not say «God doesn't exist» or «God is evil.» He asks a leading question that slightly distorts what God actually said. «Did God say you shall not eat from any tree?» No — God said you may freely eat of every tree except one. The serpent turns abundance into scarcity, generosity into restriction. Help participants recognize this tactic in their own lives. The tempter rarely announces himself with horns and a tail; he whispers doubts, reframes reality, makes sin seem reasonable and God's law seem unreasonable. This is the «Did God really say...?» dynamic that shows up in every temptation.

Eve's Addition: Adding to God's Word Is Also a Distortion

When Eve answers the serpent, she adds to God's command: «nor shall you touch it» (Gen 3:3). God never said that. This is a subtle but crucial point: we can distort God's word not only by subtracting from it but by adding to it — by making it stricter than God made it, by turning relationship into legalism. The serpent doesn't need us to deny Scripture; he just needs us to misrepresent it. Catechists: this is especially relevant when talking to people who grew up with distorted, overly strict or punitive images of God. The remedy is not to abandon God's commands but to recover their true meaning: they are protections of love, not restrictions of freedom.

The Fruit: Threefold Appeal

Genesis 3:6 describes the woman's perception of the fruit in three ways that map onto 1 John 2:16: «good for food» (the lust of the flesh), «a delight to the eyes» (the lust of the eyes), «to be desired to make one wise» (the pride of life). Temptation works through desire: it makes sin look beautiful, satisfying, and empowering. Spend a moment here. Ask: what «fruit» looks that way to you right now? The appeal is always real — sin wouldn't tempt if it didn't offer something genuinely attractive. The lie is not that the fruit looks good; it's that eating it will deliver what it promises.

God's First Question: «Where Are You?»

This is one of the most important verses in the Bible for pastoral ministry. God does not say: «What have you done?» or «How dare you?» He says: «Where are you?» He knows where they are. The question is for them — to wake them up to their condition. It is the question of a father, not a prosecutor. When catechizing, keep returning to this tone. The class on sin should not feel like an indictment; it should feel like a diagnosis that makes the cure desirable. If participants leave feeling condemned, the teaching has failed. If they leave feeling seen and offered a way out, the teaching has succeeded.

«You Are Dust»: The Entry of Death

Death enters human experience as a consequence of sin (Gen 3:19). Before the Fall, the human being was not necessarily immortal by nature, but was preserved from death by grace. The Catechism teaches: «Even though man's nature is mortal, God had destined him not to die. Death was therefore contrary to the plans of God the Creator and entered the world as a consequence of sin» (CCC 1008). This is a point many catechists gloss over. Death is not natural; it is the enemy that Christ came to destroy. The resurrection of the body is not an optional add-on; it is the reversal of the Fall's deepest wound. If you have time, connect the «garments of skins» God makes for Adam and Eve (Gen 3:21) to the first sacrifice — an animal dies so that its skin can cover human shame. The logic of sacrifice begins here and culminates at the Cross.

Original Sin: Transmitted, Not Imitated

The Catechism is precise: original sin is transmitted «by propagation, not by imitation» (CCC 404). It is not a bad habit we pick up from bad company. It is a condition of human nature we are born into — like a genetic disease that passes through generations. This distinguishes Catholic teaching from merely sociological or educational theories of sin. Sin is not just a social problem we can solve with better schools or policies. It is a wound in human nature that requires divine medicine. This also helps answer the question: «If we're baptized, why do we still sin?» Baptism removes the stain of original sin and restores sanctifying grace, but it does not remove concupiscence — the inclination toward sin that remains «for the sake of the struggle» (CCC 405). The Christian life is combat, but it is combat with the assurance of victory through Christ.

The Protoevangelium: The First Gospel

Genesis 3:15 is one of the most important verses in all of Scripture. Spend time on it. It is not an obscure prophecy; it is the hinge between the Fall and the Redemption, the seed from which the entire Old Testament grows and toward which the New Testament converges. Help participants see three levels: (1) The literal meaning in Genesis: God will maintain hostility between humanity and the serpent, and eventually humanity will defeat him. (2) The Christological fulfillment: Christ, the offspring of Mary, crushes the serpent's head definitively through his Cross and Resurrection. (3) The Marian dimension: Mary is the woman of Genesis 3:15, the new Eve, the woman of Revelation 12 clothed with the sun. If your group includes Hispanics or people familiar with Our Lady of Guadalupe, this connection is pure gold. The image on the tilma shows Mary standing on the moon, her foot on the serpent's head — the fulfillment of Genesis 3:15 rendered visible for a people who needed to see it.

The Adam-Christ Asymmetry

Emphasize the asymmetry of Romans 5:15-21: Paul uses a «much more» logic. If one man's sin brought condemnation, much more does one man's grace bring justification. «Where sin increased, grace abounded all the more.» This is not a theology of cheap grace (sin so grace may increase? Paul answers with a horrified «By no means!» in Rom 6:1-2). It is a theology of overwhelming grace: whatever evil has done, grace can do more. Whatever depth sin has reached, mercy reaches deeper. This is the note you want participants to leave with. The class should make sin feel serious — and grace feel more serious.

Melkor's Discord: Providence and the Problem of Evil

Tolkien's Ainulindalë offers one of the most profound theological images for understanding how God relates to evil. Melkor (the Satan-figure) introduces discord into the divine music. Each time, Ilúvatar (God) weaves that discord into a new, more beautiful theme. Evil is real — Melkor really chooses it — but it does not escape God's providence. The Catechism says something remarkably similar: «God is in no way, directly or indirectly, the cause of moral evil. He permits it, however, because he respects the freedom of his creatures and, mysteriously, knows how to derive good from it» (CCC 311). This is the deepest answer the Church offers to the problem of evil: not a logical solution, but a providence that is stronger than evil and weaves even sin into a greater story of redemption. The felix culpa is the ultimate expression of this: sin brought Christ into the world. Not that sin was good — but God's response to sin was so great that we dare to call the fault «happy.»

Age Adaptation

🌱 Seed (ages 7-12)

Focus: God made us special; we chose badly; God never stopped loving us.

Tell the story of Adam and Eve as a beautiful story — the garden, the fruit, the hiding. Act it out with simple props if possible. The broken mirror is perfect for this age: let them draw it and color each fragment. Focus on the Protoevangelium as a promise: God promised a hero would come to defeat the bad guy (the serpent), and that hero was Jesus. Mary is the special mom who said yes. Activities: draw the garden before and after, color the broken mirror, memorize Romans 5:20 with hand motions, write three things they can do today «out of love.» Avoid heavy theological language; emphasize that God still loved Adam and Eve even when they hid, and God still loves them.

🌿 Sprouts (ages 13-17)

Focus: Temptation is real; the «Did God really say?» voice shows up everywhere; you are not your sin.

Teenagers live daily with the serpent's whisper — social media, peer pressure, the cultural message that you decide what's right for you. Connect Genesis 3 directly to their world. «Did God really say you shouldn't sleep with your boyfriend? Did God really say you need to go to Mass? Did God really say the body matters?» Help them name the «serpents» in their lives. The Exorcist reference may resonate with horror-movie fans — use it to make the point that evil is not a metaphor. The Adam-Christ connection matters: sin entered through one person (Adam), but salvation entered through one person (Jesus). You are defined not by your worst moment but by whose you are. Mary as new Eve: the girl who said yes is their model. Activity: the «Did God Really Say?» journal. Encourage them to track the serpent's voice for one week and answer each distortion with truth.

🪴 Root (adults new to the faith)

Focus: Original sin explains why the world is broken and why we need a Savior. Grace is greater than the past.

Many adults carry guilt and shame from their past. Help them see that sin does not have the last word. Saint Augustine is perfect here: he lived sin deeply and was found by grace completely. The letter to God («Lord, I hid myself because...») can be very powerful for this group. Emphasize that original sin is not personal guilt but a condition — they are not being blamed for Adam's sin. The «long defeat» language helps them name generational patterns they may have suffered. And the eucatastrophe gives them hope: God's grace can break any pattern, heal any wound. The Marian dimension (Guadalupe as the Woman crushing the serpent) resonates deeply for Hispanic adults. Be ready for emotional responses; this topic touches deep wounds. The catechist's tone should be gentle and priestly.

🌳 Tree (formed adults)

Focus: Deep theology of original sin, concupiscence, and the Adam-Christ typology. Historical and systematic dimensions.

Go deeper into: the distinction between original sin as peccatum originale originans (the originating sin of Adam) and peccatum originale originatum (the sinful condition inherited); the concept of solidarity in Adam and the monogenism-polygenism question (CCC 390, Pius XII's Humani Generis); the nature of concupiscence as fomes peccati (the tinder for sin) and its relationship to concupiscence in the baptized; the Church Fathers: Irenaeus on recapitulation, Augustine on massa damnata, Anselm on satisfaction; the development of the Marian interpretation of Genesis 3:15 from Irenaeus through the Vulgate (ipsa vs. ipsum) to Ineffabilis Deus and Vatican II. Tolkien's framework can be explored philosophically: the long defeat as the Augustinian civitas terrena, eucatastrophe as the eschatological irruption of grace. The Exorcist opens up the theology of spiritual combat and angelology/demonology. Connect to Vatican II's Gaudium et Spes 13: sin as the division within man himself. For this group, assign additional reading: Augustine's Confessions Books I-II and VII-VIII, Tolkien's Athrabeth Finrod ah Andreth (the debate on death and the Fall among the Elves), and perhaps Milton's Paradise Lost Book IX.

Cultural Resources for the Catechist

John Milton, Paradise Lost (1667)

How to use it: Read aloud a short passage from Book IX (the Temptation) or Book XII (Adam's vision of redemption and the felix culpa). Milton's Satan is a masterclass in the psychology of sin: proud, self-deceived, unable to repent because he cannot bear to be forgiven. His famous line «Better to reign in Hell than serve in Heaven» is sin in its purest form: the preference for one's own autonomy over communion with God, even at the cost of eternal misery. After reading Satan, read Adam's response in Book XII when he learns that a Redeemer will come out of his sin: «O goodness infinite, goodness immense! / That all this good of evil shall produce, / And evil turn to good.» This is the perfect literary expression of the O felix culpa and the asymmetry of grace.

J.R.R. Tolkien, The Silmarillion

How to use it: The «Ainulindalë» (the first chapter) is a profound Catholic meditation on Genesis. Ilúvatar's response to Melkor's discord — «thou shalt see that no theme may be played that hath not its uttermost source in me, nor can any alter the music in my despite» — is the literary counterpart to the Catechism's teaching on providence and the permission of evil (CCC 309-314). If your group includes Tolkien fans, this connection is electrifying. Emphasize that Tolkien did not just write stories; he wrote a world structured by Catholic theology. The long defeat is the experience of the Elves: immortal, watching everything they love fade, fighting a war against Morgoth they know they cannot win, yet persisting in hope. The eucatastrophe is the Ring's destruction: the impossible becomes actual not through heroism but through mercy (Frodo's pity) and providence (Gollum's fall). This is the Gospel in narrative form.

Michelangelo, The Fall and Expulsion from Eden (Sistine Chapel, 1508-1512)

How to use it: Project an image or use the one in the student page. Walk participants through the contrast between the two halves. Point out how the tree dominates the center. Notice how the serpent has a woman's torso (a medieval tradition representing the devil's seductive deception). Notice Adam and Eve's beauty on the temptation side, and their horror on the expulsion side — the same people, transformed by sin. Ask: which side feels like your life right now? The theological point: sin disfigures. Grace restores beauty. The Sistine ceiling as a whole is a visual catechism moving from Creation through the Fall to the Last Judgment, and understanding any panel requires seeing it within the whole. Michelangelo was a profoundly Catholic artist; his work is not decoration but theology in paint.

Our Lady of Guadalupe, Patroness of the Americas

How to use it: For Hispanic participants, this is essential. Explain the tilma's symbols: the Virgin stands on the crescent moon, her foot on the dark angel's head — crushing the serpent as Genesis 3:15 promised. For the Aztecs and other indigenous peoples, the serpent (coatl) was the central symbol of the religion that demanded human sacrifice to appease the dark gods. The tilma proclaims in the language of indigenous symbols: the Woman whose Son crushes the serpent has arrived; the old gods are defeated; the true God has come. And she comes as a mestiza, dark-skinned, speaking Nahuatl, calling San Juan Diego by name: «Juanito, Juan Dieguito, the smallest of my sons.» She says: «Am I not here, I who am your mother?» This is Mary as the new Eve in the most culturally specific and theologically powerful expression the Americas have known. The catechist who can tell this story well will move hearts. If possible, display an image of the tilma and walk through the symbolism.

The Exorcist (1973) and the Reality of Evil

How to use it: The temptation today is to treat the devil as a metaphor, a personification of human evil with no real existence. The Catechism disagrees: «Behind the disobedient choice of our first parents lurks a seductive voice, opposed to God, which makes them fall into death out of envy. Scripture and the Church's Tradition see in this being a fallen angel, called ‘Satan’ or the ‘devil’» (CCC 391). The Exorcist (especially for adults) brings this reality home with the force of narrative. Emphasize the film's theological seriousness: it was written by a devout Catholic (William Peter Blatty) who wanted to make the reality of evil and the necessity of faith unignorable. Father Karras is a modern skeptic who rediscovers the supernatural through confrontation with irreducible evil. His sacrificial death is a layman's participation in the Cross. Use the film not to sensationalize evil but to insist on its reality and the greater power of Christ. The key pastoral point: the devil is a defeated enemy. Christ has already won. The exorcist fights from victory, not for victory.

Supplementary Resources

  • YouCat: Questions 53-70 («Why did God create man?», «What is the soul?», «What is original sin?», «How does Adam's sin affect us?», «Did God predestine some to hell?»)
  • CCC Deep Dive: §§385-421 (The Fall), §§396-412 (Original Sin), §§1846-1876 (Sin: definitions and distinctions)
  • Scripture: Genesis 1-3; Romans 5:12-21; Romans 7:14-25; Psalm 51; Wisdom 2:23-24; Revelation 12; Luke 1:26-38
  • Church Fathers: Irenaeus, Adversus Haereses III (recapitulation, Mary as new Eve); Augustine, De Civitate Dei XIII-XIV (original sin, the two loves); Augustine, Confessions I-II, VII-VIII (the experience of sin and grace)
  • Video: «The Fall» — episode from «The Bible» miniseries (History Channel); Bishop Barron's «Catholicism» series episodes on the problem of evil and the Cross; «Guadalupe: The Miracle and the Message» (2015 documentary)
  • Music: «Tú Reinas» by Hakuna Group Music («where sin increased, grace abounded all the more»); «O Happy Fault» («O felix culpa») from the Exsultet setting at Easter Vigil; «Ave Maria» settings by Schubert or Arcadelt
  • Art: Masaccio, Expulsion from the Garden of Eden (Brancacci Chapel, Florence — arguably the most powerful single image of the Fall in Western art); Hieronymus Bosch, The Garden of Earthly Delights (left panel: Eden before the Fall; central panel: the world given over to sin; right panel: Hell); Gustave Doré's illustrations for Paradise Lost; the tilma of Our Lady of Guadalupe
  • Literature: John Milton, Paradise Lost (Books I, IV, IX, XII); J.R.R. Tolkien, The Silmarillion («Ainulindalë» and «Of the Beginning of Days»); Fyodor Dostoevsky, The Brothers Karamazov (Book V: Rebellion and The Grand Inquisitor); C.S. Lewis, The Problem of Pain and The Screwtape Letters; Flannery O'Connor, short stories («A Good Man Is Hard to Find,» «Revelation») — Flannery O'Connor is the greatest Catholic writer on sin and grace in the 20th century

Difficult Questions

«If God is good, why did he create the devil?»

God did not create the devil as an evil being. The devil and the demons were created as good angels who freely and irrevocably rejected God (CCC 391-395). Their sin cannot be forgiven not because God refuses mercy, but because their choice — made with the full clarity of angelic knowledge and without the weakness of a body — is irrevocable by its very nature. The serpent in Genesis 3 is a creature that the devil uses, not himself a fallen angel in serpent-form. The catechist should emphasize: God creates only good. Evil is the parasite, the privation, the distortion of what was made good. The question is not «why did God create evil?» but «why did God create beings with the freedom to reject him?» And the answer is that love without freedom is not love.

«Is it fair that we all bear Adam's sin?»

Original sin is not personal guilt. It is a sinful condition, not a sinful act. Saint Paul's Adam-Christ parallel in Romans 5:12-21 is the key: just as all humanity is united to Adam by natural generation and shares in the consequences of his sin, so all the baptized are united to Christ by supernatural regeneration and share in the grace of his redemption. We are not guilty of Adam's sin, but we are born into a human nature that his sin wounded. Think of it like an inheritance: a child may inherit a parent's debt without being morally responsible for incurring it. The analogy is imperfect — original sin is more than debt, it is a wound — but it helps. Most importantly: the doctrine of original sin is not a reason for despair but the explanation for why we need a Savior. The fairness question has its answer in the greater asymmetry: if it seems unfair that we share in Adam's fall, how much more unfair — how much more gratuitous — that we can share in Christ's redemption. The Church's answer is not to deny the solidarity in Adam but to proclaim the greater solidarity in Christ.

«Is the Eden account historical or symbolic?»

The Catechism says that the Genesis account uses «figurative language» to describe a «primo-genital» event that really occurred (CCC 390). The narrative form is symbolic — a talking serpent, magical trees, a garden that is clearly an archetype rather than a geographical location — but the event is real: there was a first human pair (monogenism) who sinned freely, and that sin had real consequences for all humanity. This is not a fundamentalist literalism that reads Genesis like a newspaper; it is a properly Catholic approach that takes the text's literary form seriously while affirming its historical substance. The Council of Trent defined that Adam's sin is transmitted to all his descendants by propagation, not imitation, and the loss of original justice is real, not metaphorical (DS 1511-1512). The catechist can acknowledge the symbolic elements while affirming the reality of the Fall. The key pastoral move: the story is true even if not literal. What matters is not whether the fruit was an apple but whether humanity really chose against God and really suffered the consequences.

«If baptism removes original sin, why do we keep sinning?»

Baptism removes the stain of original sin and restores sanctifying grace, but it does not eliminate concupiscence — the inclination toward sin that remains «for the sake of the struggle» (CCC 405, quoting the Council of Trent). This is not a failure of baptism; it is part of God's design for our sanctification. The struggle against concupiscence, waged with the help of grace, is how we grow in virtue and merit. Think of it like physical therapy after a serious injury: the injury (original sin) is healed, but the weakness it left behind (concupiscence) requires ongoing work to overcome. Baptism gives us the grace to fight; it does not make the fight unnecessary. Saint Paul describes this dynamic in Romans 7 (the struggle) and Romans 8 (the victory through the Holy Spirit). The Christian life is combat, but it is combat with the assurance of victory. As Paul concludes: «I can do all things through him who strengthens me» (Phil 4:13). The key pastoral point: if you're struggling, you're not failing; you're fighting. And Christ fights with you.

«If God knew we would sin, why did he create us?»

This is the deepest form of the problem of evil. The Church's answer is not a logical deduction but a proclamation of the felix culpa: God permitted sin because, in his infinite wisdom and love, he knew he could bring a greater good out of it — the Incarnation, the Cross, the Resurrection, the deification of humanity through Christ. The Catechism says: «God permits evil in order to draw forth some greater good. Thus Saint Paul says, ‘Where sin increased, grace abounded all the more’ (Rom 5:20). And the Exsultet sings: ‘O happy fault... which gained for us so great a Redeemer!’» (CCC 412). This is not a philosophical solution that satisfies every rational objection; it is a theological mystery we accept in faith. The world with sin-and-redemption is, in God's mysterious calculus, more glorious than a world without sin would have been. The Cross is not Plan B. The Lamb was «slain from the foundation of the world» (Rev 13:8). God's permission of sin was always ordered toward the greater gift of Christ. The catechist should not pretend this answers all questions; it doesn't. But it does point toward the answer, which is not a theory but a Person.

«How do we reconcile evolution with original sin?»

The Church does not oppose the theory of evolution as a scientific account of biological origins, provided it does not deny the special creation of the human soul or divine providence (Humani Generis, 1950). The difficulty concerns original sin: if humanity emerged gradually from a population, was there a single «first human pair» who sinned? The Catechism and the Council of Trent affirm monogenism — a single first couple from whom all humanity descends and from whom original sin is transmitted. Some theologians have proposed models that reconcile polygenism with original sin (e.g., original sin as the universal sinfulness of humanity at its origins), but these remain theological speculation, not magisterial teaching. The catechist, unless addressing a Tree-level group, can simply note that the Church affirms both the truth of Scripture's teaching on the Fall and the legitimate findings of science, without needing to resolve every tension in a 60-minute class. The key is to affirm that whatever the details of human origins, humanity really did choose against God, and that choice has real consequences.

Catechist's Prayer

Lord, you who created us in your image
and did not grow weary of seeking us when we hid among the trees,
give me a compassionate heart for those who listen to me.
Help me to show that sin does not have the last word,
that your mercy is greater than our frailty,
that where sin increased, grace abounded all the more.
May my words not be condemnation
but a bridge to your love.
Let me speak the truth about sin with clarity,
and the truth about grace with joy.
May Mary, the new Eve, the Woman who crushes the serpent,
intercede for me and for those I teach,
that we may stop hiding and let ourselves be found
by the Father who has been calling our name since Eden.
Amen.

Print Catechist's Guide
